metadata.summary: RASA (Receive, Appreciate, Summarize, Ask) listening practice that ensures the speaker feels understood; reaches Level 5 empathic listening.

content.title: Active Listening
content.summary: A structured listening practice (RASA: Receive, Appreciate, Summarize, Ask) that ensures the speaker feels understood before the listener responds. Combines paraphrasing, mirroring, open questions, and empathic reflection to reach Level 5 empathic listening — understanding both content and emotion.

why.p: People who don't feel heard withhold information, resist decisions, and disengage. Active listening is the single highest-leverage technique for requirements gathering and conflict de-escalation: it costs nothing and directly reduces the "missing information" failure mode. Research shows 80% of communication problems trace to listening failures, not speaking failures.

list.item: Requirements gathering sessions with stakeholders or clients
1-on-1 meetings where trust or engagement is at risk
Customer discovery interviews (especially with skeptical interviewees)
Conflict situations where one party feels misunderstood
Performance discussions or coaching conversations
Generating interview scripts that follow RASA: open questions, paraphrase prompts, silence cues
Coaching prep: producing reflective-listening response starters keyed to a specific emotional context
Transcript post-processing: identifying where the interviewer talked too much (>20% of words) or used closed questions
Requirements gathering support: converting a stakeholder conversation into structured observations using RASA labels
Real-time agent conversations — agents cannot perform empathic listening live; they can scaffold it but not execute it
High-stakes interpersonal conflicts — a script-driven approach risks feeling mechanical and backfiring
Situations where a direct answer is needed and the other party is not emotionally engaged
Asynchronous text threads where silence and pace cues are absent
Replacing empathic presence: the Level 5 "empathic listening" in the framework requires reading emotional state from tone and body language, which text-based agents cannot do from transcripts alone

section.name: rasa-framework
section.p: The RASA model (Receive, Appreciate, Summarize, Ask), the five empathic listening levels, and the reflective listening formula for communicating both content and emotional understanding.

rule.statement: Work through all four RASA steps in every substantive conversation: Receive (full attention), Appreciate (acknowledgment), Summarize (reflect back), Ask (open follow-up).
rule.rationale: Skipping Summarize is the most common failure — without paraphrasing, the speaker cannot confirm their message was understood and will either repeat or withhold detail.

example.note: Requirements session with a skeptical stakeholder.
example.code: R: No phone, eye contact, note-taking only. A: "I see", nods, "Go on..." S: "So if I understand you, the current process takes 4 hours because the data export step is manual — is that right?" A: "Can you walk me through what happens after the export?"

rule.statement: Use What, How, and Tell-me-about openers instead of Is, Do, or Did — closed questions produce yes/no answers that close down exploration.
rule.rationale: Open questions give the speaker control of the answer scope, which surfaces information the interviewer did not know to ask about.

example.code: "Is the system slow?" "Do you like the design?" "Did that cause a problem?"
"How does the system perform under load?" "What do you think about the design?" "How did that affect your team?"

rule.statement: Target Level 5 (Empathic) in high-stakes conversations: understand both the content of what is said and the emotional state of the speaker.
rule.rationale: Levels 1-4 process words; Level 5 processes meaning and feeling. Decision-makers and clients operating under stress communicate 40-60% of their actual concern through emotional register, not explicit words.

list.item: Level 1 — Ignoring: not listening at all
Level 2 — Pretending: "uh-huh" with no engagement
Level 3 — Selective: hearing only parts while forming a reply
Level 4 — Attentive: focusing on words, taking notes
Level 5 — Empathic: understanding feeling AND content

rule.statement: Reflect both content and feeling using the formula: "It sounds like you're [FEELING] because [CONTENT]."
rule.rationale: Naming the feeling signals that you are operating at Level 5; the speaker either confirms (and feels understood) or corrects (providing higher-quality signal).

example.code: "It sounds like you're frustrated because the deadline keeps moving." "It seems like you're excited about this new approach." "I hear that you're concerned about the budget implications."

rule.statement: Mirror by repeating the last 1-3 words of the speaker's sentence as a question — do not add interpretation.
rule.rationale: Mirroring is the lowest-friction technique that produces the highest elaboration rate. It signals attention without leading the speaker toward a particular answer.

example.code: Speaker: "We're really struggling with the deployment process." You: "The deployment process?" Speaker: [elaborates with root cause and context]

rule.statement: Pause 3-5 seconds after the speaker finishes before responding — do not fill silence immediately.
rule.rationale: People add the most important information after the first pause. Immediate response signals that you were waiting to speak, not listening.

rule.statement: Paraphrase at least once per conversation using: "If I understand correctly, you mean..."
rule.rationale: Paraphrasing forces the listener to synthesize rather than just hear, and gives the speaker an explicit correction opportunity before assumptions harden.

rule.statement: Identify and neutralize the five common barriers before entering a high-stakes listening situation.
rule.rationale: Each barrier converts a potential Level 5 interaction into Level 3 or lower, degrading information quality and trust.

list.item: Planning response — fix: commit to understanding before forming any reply
Judging — fix: suspend judgment until the speaker has fully finished
Interrupting — fix: wait 2 seconds after they stop before speaking
Advising prematurely — fix: ask "Do you want advice or just to vent?" before offering solutions
Distractions — fix: remove phone, close laptop before the conversation begins

section.name: agent-workflow-patterns

rule.statement: Use agents for pre-conversation scaffolding and post-conversation analysis, not live listening execution.
rule.rationale: Active listening is fundamentally reactive to real-time emotional signals. Agents can generate question scripts and annotate transcripts, but cannot adapt in real time to tone or body language.

example.note: Two-pass agent workflow for a requirements session.
example.code:
Pass 1 (before): Generate RASA-structured question flow for the session goal. - Output: Receive-phase opener, Appreciate phrases, Summarize starters, 5 open Ask questions. - Constraint: no yes/no questions, no "Is..." or "Do you..." openers.
Pass 2 (after): Annotate transcript with RASA labels. - Flag: closed questions used by interviewer - Flag: interviewer word-share > 20% in any 3-turn window - Flag: Summarize step skipped

section.p: A subagent receives a conversation goal (e.g., "requirements gathering session with a skeptical stakeholder") and outputs: (1) a RASA-structured question flow, (2) 5-10 paraphrase/reflection starters tuned to expected emotional register, (3) a closed-to-open question conversion for any pre-existing question list. After the human conducts the conversation, the agent receives the transcript and annotates it: each turn labeled by RASA step, word-count ratio check, and barrier identification.

Recommended subagents: General Claude Sonnet call — question generation and transcript annotation; sufficient for RASA tagging. faion (communicator domain) — for cross-methodology context (mom-test, stakeholder-communication).

section.name: prompt-patterns
section.prompt: Generate a RASA-structured question flow for: <CONVERSATION_GOAL>. Interviewee profile: <ROLE/CONTEXT>. Output format: - Receive: [opening, set attention] - Appreciate: [acknowledgment phrases] - Summarize: [paraphrase starters] - Ask: [5 open follow-up questions] No yes/no questions. No "Is..." or "Do you..." openers.

Annotate this conversation transcript. For each interviewer turn: label as R/A/S/A (RASA), note if it's a closed question, note if the interviewer speaks more than the interviewee in any 3-turn window. Transcript: <TRANSCRIPT>

section.name: cli-tools-and-services
section.p: CLI tools and services that support transcript-based active listening workflows.

list.item: whisper (OpenAI) — Transcribe recorded conversations for post-analysis. Install: pip install openai-whisper
pyannote.audio — Speaker diarization to separate interviewer vs. interviewee turns. Install: pip install pyannote.audio
nltk — Word count and sentence type (interrogative) detection on transcripts. Install: pip install nltk
Otter.ai (SaaS, REST API) — Speaker-labeled transcripts; agent can parse speaker turn ratios
Dovetail (SaaS, partial REST API) — Tag transcript highlights with RASA labels
Loom (SaaS, no API for transcript) — Video recording; use third-party transcription then agent analysis
Fireflies.ai (SaaS, REST API) — Auto-transcription + meeting search; good for post-session analysis
Build the question script before the conversation, but mark questions as "adaptable" — the script is a scaffold, not a reading list.
For requirements gathering, use Mirroring (repeat last 1-3 words as a question) as a low-effort, high-yield technique; it is the easiest for a human to apply naturally without a script.
Run the "closed-to-open" conversion on any existing requirement spec questions before a stakeholder session — most specs contain implicit yes/no questions.
After the conversation, use the transcript annotation pass to identify which RASA steps were skipped (usually Summarize); feed that gap back into coaching notes.
The "ask for advice or just vent?" technique from the Barriers section prevents misaligned responses; make it a default opener in sensitive conversations.

Agents generating "open" questions often produce questions that start with "What" or "How" but are still leading ("What problems did you have with X?" implies X caused problems). Check for embedded assumptions.
Transcript word-count analysis does not account for sentence density — a human may say 20 words but pack in 3x the information of an agent's 60-word turn.
Empathic reflection statements generated without knowing the interviewee's emotional context can land as condescending or inaccurate ("It sounds like you're frustrated..." when they were calm).
Human checkpoint required: before acting on a synthesized requirements list derived from a transcript, a human must validate that the RASA annotations match the actual intent of the conversation.

Silence (the 3-5 second pause) is the most effective active listening tool and the one completely absent from agent-driven text exchanges; compensate with explicit "Is there anything you'd like to add?" prompts.

antipattern.description: Generating empathic reflection statements without knowing the interviewee's emotional context.
antipattern.reason: Statements like "It sounds like you're frustrated..." land as condescending or inaccurate when the person was calm. Reflection statements must respond to observed signals, not be pre-generated.

antipattern.description: Using "What" or "How" openers for questions that still embed assumptions.
antipattern.reason: "What problems did you have with X?" assumes X caused problems. Check every open question for embedded assumptions before the session.

section.name: word-ratio-checker

template.purpose: Prompt to generate a RASA-structured interview script for a given goal
Prompt to annotate a transcript with RASA labels and quality flags
Python snippet to measure interviewer word-share from a transcript
